sections.title: Overview
sections.content: A vacuum mattress provides supportive spinal motion restriction and full-body stabilisation while being more comfortable and lower-risk than prolonged transport on a hard board. It is mainly a transport and packaging device rather than a reason to delay more urgent care.

sections.title: Procedure
sections.content:
1. Prepare the mattress before moving the patient onto it

2. Transfer the patient using the lowest-movement technique appropriate to the situation

3. Mould the mattress around the patient to support alignment and comfort, then evacuate air until it is firm

4. Secure the patient sufficiently for transport without creating avoidable restriction or airway issues

5. Reassess comfort, neurological status, and package stability after setup and after major moves

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Recheck rigidity during transport and after transfers because vacuum can be lost
- Use additional padding or adjustment to maintain the best achievable alignment and airway access
- A vacuum mattress is usually preferred for transport, but not at the cost of delayed time-critical care
- Document why it was used and how the patient tolerated it
